coredata.dc:title: Solvatochromism provoked multi-emissive polyethylenimine assimilated carbon dots for fluorescence sensing and adsorptive removal of copper and bismuth ions in aqua regions

coredata.dc:creator: Z, Nawas Sherif
K, Anitha
coredata.dc:description: This contemporary research investigation uses polyethylenimine-assimilated carbon quantum dots (PC) to aid in the fluorescence sensing and adsorptive removal of copper (Cu) and bismuth (Bi) metal ions from the contaminated, impure water. The fluorescence response of Cu and Bi ions to orange and red emissive carbon dots (OPC and RPC, respectively) serves to identify the ions. Here, the dual emissions of PC are achieved while dispersing it in water (OPC) and methanol (RPC) based on the solvatochromism effect. This encourages PC to sense and remove multiple metal ions. Here, the sensing of copper and bismuth ions is supported by the "Turn-Off" sensing mechanism. Moreover, the adsorption process removes both metal ions. The selectivity experiment is well-proven with the interference of single and multiple metal ions. Furthermore, the prepared samples are optimized with pH, time and concentration of both materials and metal ions, to achieve high efficiency. By following, the reusability of both OPC and RPC is tested for about three cycles. Then, the mechanism of sensing and removal is elaborated with several analyses, such as zeta potential measurements, lifetime decay, determining quenching constants, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy analysis. Lastly, the practical ability of the material is analysed with three actual environmental samples.

head.ce:title: Solvatochromism provoked multi-emissive polyethylenimine assimilated carbon dots for fluorescence sensing and adsorptive removal of copper and bismuth ions in aqua regions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, 625021, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, 625021, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This contemporary research investigation uses polyethylenimine-assimilated carbon quantum dots (PC) to aid in the fluorescence sensing and adsorptive removal of copper (Cu) and bismuth (Bi) metal ions from the contaminated, impure water. The fluorescence response of Cu and Bi ions to orange and red emissive carbon dots (OPC and RPC, respectively) serves to identify the ions. Here, the dual emissions of PC are achieved while dispersing it in water (OPC) and methanol (RPC) based on the solvatochromism effect. This encourages PC to sense and remove multiple metal ions. Here, the sensing of copper and bismuth ions is supported by the "Turn-Off" sensing mechanism. Moreover, the adsorption process removes both metal ions. The selectivity experiment is well-proven with the interference of single and multiple metal ions. Furthermore, the prepared samples are optimized with pH, time and concentration of both materials and metal ions, to achieve high efficiency. By following, the reusability of both OPC and RPC is tested for about three cycles. Then, the mechanism of sensing and removal is elaborated with several analyses, such as zeta potential measurements, lifetime decay, determining quenching constants, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy analysis. Lastly, the practical ability of the material is analysed with three actual environmental samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Preventing water from getting polluted is the primary environmental concern because it is the most important natural resource for all living things (Zamora-Ledezma et al., 2021). On the other hand, waste is released into the water due to the rapid development of various entities. Among all other contaminants, heavy metal ion contamination is preferred to be the most noxious due to its non-biodegradable nature (Fu and Wang, 2011). In particular, prolonged excessive copper exposure leads to neurodegenerative disorders like Alzheimer's disease (Wang and Zong, 2015), along with usual illnesses like stomach aches and headaches. It has an impact on the organism's physiology (Zheng et al., 2019). Bismuth ions are hazardous to fauna and also to flora (Salam et al., 2021). Thus, it is necessary to identify and eliminate heavy metals like copper and bismuth from the contaminated water.

In this work, heavy metal ions are detected via optical fluorescence sensing because of their unique attributes, such as quick reaction times, low costs, and an easy method that doesn't require a professional (De Acha et al., 2019). After that, the Heavy metal ions are removed by utilizing the adsorption method, over other removal techniques like ion exchange, membrane filtration, and chemical precipitation, because the adsorption method is simple, highly efficient, leaves little residue, and is environmentally favourable for the removal of heavy metal ions (Ayub et al., 2020).

This work utilises semiconducting quantum dots to detect and eliminate metal ions while taking into account a number of exclusive properties, which include high surface-to-volume ratio, low photobleaching, tunable emission spectra, broad excitation spectra, affordability, and ease of use (Chen and Zhu, 2005; Zhang et al., 2014). Here, carbon quantum dots have an advantage owing to their low toxicity (Yarur et al., 2019; Qureshi et al., 2024) over other materials that have been reported, such as quinolone derivatives and 1,8-naphthalimide derivatives reported by Wang et al. and Zhifeng Xu et al., as a fluorescent probe to detect copper ions (Wang and Zong, 2015; Xu et al., 2018). Zhang et al. use rhodamine 6G to detect bismuth ions (Zhang et al., 2018), while some adsorbents, such as nanoparticles of zero-valent iron by Shahwan et al. (Karabelli et al., 2008) and graphite modified with gallic acid material by Biljana et al., to remove bismuth ions (Vasić et al., 2012). Furthermore, the carbon dots were functionalized with polyethylenimine to overcome some limitations such as aggregation, solubility and biocompatibility with the aid of amine groups in polyethylenimine (Zeng et al., 2018; Khan and Patil, 2021; Liu et al., 2017). Then, polyethylenimine functionalized carbon dots were established with solvatochromic effect by dispersing them in different solvents to enhance their single emission into a dual emissive nature (Wu et al., 2019). By using dual emissions (orange and red), this work's most significant innovation is the promotion of a single material for dual applications, detection and removal of two different metal ions (bismuth and copper), as opposed to perform individually.

In this paper, carbon quantum dots assimilated by polyethylenimine (PC) are synthesized, characterized, and dispersed with different solvents to examine their solvatochromic effect, and applied for fluorescence sensing and adsorptive removal of copper and bismuth ions. In addition, its detection limit, adsorption efficiency, sensing and removal mechanisms and actual environmental sample analysis are investigated.

Citric acid (CA), Urea, polyethylenimine (PEI) solution (Molecular weight of 1800 g/mol), phosphate standard for IC–buffer solution, barium chloride dihydrate, bismuth (III) chloride, cadmium acetate dihydrate, chromium (III) chloride hexahydrate, copper (II) chloride dihydrate, cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate, iron (II) sulphate heptahydrate, mercury (II) chloride, magnesium chloride hexahydrate, manganese (II) chloride tetrahydrate, nickel (II) acetate, lead nitrate, tin (II) chloride, zinc sulphate heptahydrate, distilled water, iso-propyl alcohol (IPA), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), methanol, ethanol, sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid were purchased in analytical grade from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Private Limited in Bangalore, India, and were utilized without further purification.

The prepared substance is characterised using the following instruments: The powder XRD patterns were obtained using a PANalytical X-pert pro diffractometer with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.540 Å) operating at a voltage of 40 kV and a current of 30 mA. The SHIMADZU UV-2450 spectrophotometer was used to record the absorption spectra and utilized to perform the adsorption experiments; the SHIMADZU RF-5301 spectrofluorometer was used to record the photoluminescence spectra, and to perform the sensing experiments; and the SHIMADZU FTIR-8400S was utilized to record the Fourier transform infrared data. The materials' morphology was investigated using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN TEM, 200 kV). The elemental composition of the samples was verified using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), and analysis of the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was made using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha-KAN9954133) with an energy range of 100–4000 eV. The JOBIN YUON Fluorolog-3-11 (Pulsed-Diode source; 340 nm) was utilized to evaluate the fluorophore's lifetime, and the Malvern Zetasizer MAL1052893 was utilized to determine its zeta potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of polyethylenimine assimilated carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Polyethylenimine assimilated carbon dots (PC) was synthesized via a hydrothermal process by dissolving 1.26 g of citric acid, 1.37 g of urea, and 0.18 g of polyethylenimine solution in 30 ml of DMF. Then the solution was stirred for 10 min to achieve homogeneous solution (Yu et al., 2022). Subsequently, the solution is placed in an autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for a duration of 4 h. The resulting dark solution of PC with a concentration of 1 g/l is dispersed in water and methanol separately once it has reached room temperature to produce OPC and RPC, respectively. A 0.22 μm filter is thus used to filter the dissolved material in order to remove unreacted particles, and it is then kept for further experimental usage. The schematic representation of this synthesis method is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of metal ions into OPC and RPC
ce:section.ce:para: To identify and eliminate metal ions from the water samples, metal ions have to be incorporated into the prepared OPC and RPC materials. To achieve this, 3 ml of OPC and RPC materials are added to a buffer solution of pH 7 (10 mM) at different concentrations and kept separately. Next, 60 μl of each metal ion stock solution was added, and samples were permitted to record absorbance and fluorescence spectra (Liu et al., 2018).

Optimization entails adjusting variables like pH, metal ion concentration, material concentration (OPC and RPC), etc. pH variation refers to adjusting the solution's pH after adding metal ions as previously mentioned. Here, sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid are utilized to adjust the pH of the solution. When making stock solutions of the appropriate metal ions at a specific concentration, the concentration of metal ions is first altered before being incorporated into the material. Lastly, before adding the metal ions, the concentrations of OPC and RPC are adjusted by simply dissolving PC in the appropriate amount of water and methanol solvent (Radhakrishnan et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation for real environmental sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Water from nearby rivers, wells, and taps was collected. To eliminate suspended undesirable particles, a 0.22 μm filter was used to filter the collected water samples. The metal ion incorporation method was used to introduce copper and bismuth ions in filtered water samples at different amounts, such as 0.5 μM and 1.5 μM, allowing for investigation (Liu et al., 2018).

The diffraction peak of prepared polyethylenimine-assimilated carbon dots appears at 23.2° (Fig. 2 (a)), indicating that PC is naturally amorphous and belongs to (002) plane (Ding et al., 2017). This indicates the disorderliness of PC. The observed broad diffraction peak suggests particle sizes in the lower nanometer range (Khan et al., 2017).

Transmission electron microscopy with high resolution is used to analyse the morphology of OPC and RPC (Fig. 2(b and c)). As prepared, OPC and RPC have a spherical morphology with average particle sizes of roughly 7.2 nm and 8.5 nm, respectively (Fig. 2(d) and (e)) (Li et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2019). The approximation stated with the broad diffraction peak in PXRD data is confirmed by the reduced dimension of OPC and RPC particles.

Fig. 3 (a) and (b) show the absorbance spectra of OPC and RPC. OPC and RPC, thus, show two absorption peaks at 350 nm and another broad peak at 450–515 nm. The n-π∗ transition of C=O and C-N groups is responsible for these peaks (Sun et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2017). The band gap energy of OPC and RPC is calculated using the Tauc plot from the absorbance spectra. As a result, the band gap energy for OPC is 2.37 eV, and for RPC is 2.34 eV (see the inset of Fig. 3(a) and (b)).

The photoluminescence spectra of OPC and RPC are shown in Fig. 2(c and d). When excited at 540 nm, OPC exhibits a strong emission at 580 nm (Ma et al., 2020), while RPC exhibits an emission at 640 nm when excited at 600 nm (Sun et al., 2022). As a result, when exposed to UV light, OPC emits a fluorescent orange colour and RPC emits a red colour (see Fig. 3(c, d) inset).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lifetime and quantum yield of fluorophore
ce:section.ce:para: The lifetime spectra of prepared materials and materials with metal ions are shown in Fig. 3(e and f). OPC's average lifetime decay is 1.3 × 10−9s, while RPC's average lifetime decay is 2.16 × 10−9s. The average lifetime of similar materials like RPC and OPC drops to 1.7 × 10−9s and 9.8 × 10−10s, respectively, when metal ions like bismuth and copper are added. The fluorescence quenching (Turn-Off) reaction of metal ions is represented by a decrease in lifetime following metal ion incorporation (Huang et al., 2018). Then the quantum yield of OPC and RPC is determined as 77.4 % and 69.6 %, respectively. Thus, both OPC and RPC possess high quantum yields.

FTIR analysis is used to study the functional groups of OPC and RPC. The FTIR spectra have been recorded in the region 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 (Fig. 4 (a)). N-H and O-H stretching vibrations are represented by the peak at 3147 cm−1 (Yang et al., 2017; Zan et al., 2020). At 2924 cm−1, the C-H vibration peak is seen. The peak at 2059 cm−1 becomes observable when atmospheric CO is present (Zheng et al., 2021). The stretching vibration of C=O is the cause of the peak at 1712 cm−1 (Hoan et al., 2019). The peak at 1665 cm−1 confirms the presence of C=O and C=N stretching vibration (Xu et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019). C-N stretching vibration is attributed to the peak at 1396 cm−1 (Mu et al., 2020). The bending vibration of C-H is associated with the peak at 1195 cm−1 (Li et al., 2019), and the rise of the peaks at 1103 cm−1, 1056 cm−1, and 1018 cm−1 confirms the C-O vibration. As a result of the functionalization of polyethylenimine, the amine group (N-H bond) is formed, which helps to improve the performance of carbon dots in water (Li et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2021; Mesbah et al., 2020). Here, the presence of N-H groups and O-H groups is responsible for the solubility of the prepared carbon dots in water. Further, the O-H bond serves as a site for adsorption, thereby forming a chelation with the target metal ions (Khan et al., 2019).

Polyethyleneimine-assimilated carbon dots are subjected to surface elemental analysis using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Three main peaks have been seen in the XPS spectra at 285.1 eV, 399.4 eV, and 531.9 eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 4(b)). These peaks attest to the existence of nitrogen, oxygen, and carbon on PC's surface (Yang et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2020). Deconvolution of the C1s band, as presented in Fig. 4(c), reveals three peaks at 284.9 eV (C-C/C=C), 286.2 eV (C-N/C-O), and 288.3 eV (COOH). The C=O and C-OH/C-O-C groups report that the O1s band exhibits two peaks (Fig. 4(d)) at 531.6 eV and 533.5 eV, respectively. In the N1s peaks (Fig. 4(e)), three peaks at 398.7 eV, 399.9 eV and 400.4 eV, respectively, correspond to C-N-C, N-C and N-H (Li et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2019). XPS results also validate the significance of amine groups and hydroxyl groups for the enhancement of carbon dot's properties and interaction of the material with solvent and the chelation between the metal ions and the materials (Khan et al., 2019; Krystkowiak et al., 2006).

According to Fig. 4(f) of the EDX analysis, PC contains carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen with weight percentages of 49.34 %, 23 %, and 27.66 %, respectively. Thus, the elemental analysis concludes that the polyethylenimine-assimilated carbon dots are organic material (Farzaneh et al., 2020).

The fluorescence nature of carbon dots significantly changes with respect to solvent, stated as the solvatochromism effect (Nigam and Rutan, 2001). Thus, by using this effect, the prepared PC is used to examine its behaviour towards a solvent. The ultimate goal is to favour environmentally friendly solvents over more harmful ones. Thus, for research, it is preferable to use green solvents such as water, ethanol, methanol, and isopropyl alcohol due to their less toxic and biodegradable nature, thereby avoiding environmental implications (Jessop, 2011; Almohasin et al., 2023). The solvent's primary contribution is homogeneity, which is required to encourage the formation of hydrogen bonds between the solvent and the PC. Hence, various alcohols and water are preferred as solvents, which are all capable of forming active hydrogen bonds (Krystkowiak et al., 2006). Fig. 5 (a) illustrates the solvatochromism effect, where the fluorescence emission of PC shifts in response to different solvents. This spectral change occurs due to a solvation mechanism, when the excited fluorophore reacts with the surrounding solvent molecules. This interaction leads to shifts in the emission spectra of the fluorophore with respect to the solvent's polarity. Specifically, dissolving PC in polar protic solvents results in a redshift (Bathochromic shift) of the fluorescence emission. This spectrum shift results from the solvent's polarity and is dependent on the particular way that solvents and carbon dots interact (Mukherjee et al., 2017). However, complex interactions occur between the solvent molecules and the carbon dot's functional groups. In the carbon dot, functional groups like hydroxyl, amine, and carboxyl groups like OH, NH, and COOH encourage hydrogen bonding with the solvent. When comparing the fluorescence spectra of PC in different solvents (Fig. 5(a)), the red shift of 61 nm occurs in PC dispersed in methanol from PC dispersed in IPA, corresponding to the solvents polarity. But, PC in water shows a blue shift (hypsochromic shift) of 55 nm from PC in methanol as shown in the Fig. 5(b). More commonly, a red shift occurs due to an increase in polarity, but contradictionally, a high-polarity water exhibits a blue shift, when PC is dispersed in it. This represents the negative solvatochromism effect. The reason for this negative solvatochromism phenomenon is that the high polarity of water stabilizes the ground state of the carbon dot than the excited state (Sun et al., 2018; Yoshinaga et al., 2021). Thus, its limited stabilization of the excited state of carbon dots results in the formation of a larger band gap, which leads to a blue shift in the fluorescence spectrum.

On analysing the excitation-dependent behaviour of OPC and RPC, OPC shows its excitation-independent behaviour, and its emission does not vary significantly, but red shifting or bathochromic shifting occurs in RPC when the excitation wavelength is increased, as seen in Fig. 5(c) and (d). The different sizes of PC and the variety of functional groups on the surface of OPC and RPC are the causes of this emission behaviour (Huo et al., 2021). Therefore, when the excitation wavelength is changed, the emission peaks move towards longer wavelengths. The surface state effect on the fluorescence emission of the fluorophore is the basis for these observations. Furthermore, the presence of COOH groups and oxygen on the surface of RPC causes bathochromic changes. Lastly, the surface state emission mechanism causes RPC to exhibit bathochromic shifts as a result of their excitation-dependent emission behaviour (Mondal and Pramanik, 2023).

Based on the correlation between the band gap, colour of emission, and size of OPC and RPC, it is evident that the quantum confinement effect is the cause of the emission. According to the Tauc plot, OPC's band gap energy is 2.37 eV, while RPC's is 2.34 eV. As a result, the size of the carbon quantum dots increase as the band gap energy decreases. The HRTEM analysis's particle size distribution plot reveals an increase in OPC (7.2 nm) and RPC (8.5 nm). The effect can also be seen in the colour of the emission; red emission occurs at 640 nm and orange emission at 580 nm. The low band gap energy may also be connected to this high emission peak. As a result, based on the quantum confinement effect, the synthesized carbon dot's properties like particle size, band gap, and emission colour are associated (Tejwan et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application on sensing and removal of copper and bismuth ions
Fluorescence sensing of copper and bismuth ions
Selection of copper and bismuth ions
ce:section.ce:para: Polyethylenimine-assimilated carbon quantum dots exhibit extremely intense fluorescence behaviour when dissolved in water and methanol, emitting light at the orange and red emissive regions. In this case, it is employed to select target heavy metal ions according to its fluorescence response. The produced OPC and RPC materials are mixed individually with different metal ions, including Ba, Bi, Cd, Co, Cr, Cu, Fe, Hg, Mg, Mn, Ni, Pb, Sn, and Zn, with a known concentration of 0.5 mM and their fluorescence responses are noted. All metal ions exhibit enhanced fluorescence towards OPC and RPC, while the fluorescence intensity of the OPC (Fig. 6 (a)) and RPC (Fig. 7 (a)) is reduced by copper and bismuth ions, respectively. As a result, a fluorescence "Turn-Off" mechanism occurs. Therefore, it is possible to select copper and bismuth as the target analytes by taking into account the fluorescence behaviour of the copper-incorporated OPC material and the bismuth-incorporated RPC material. In the interference analysis, other single heavy metal ions (HMs) and multiple heavy metal ions (MHMs) are added to the working solution along with copper and bismuth ions. The Cu and Bi ions show their unique nature of turn-off fluorescence response even on reacting with other heavy metal ions. There is no interference of other metal ions with Cu and Bi ions as shown in Figs. 6(b) and 7(b). Thus the selection process is well proved based on the interference analysis of other metal ions.

Prepared OPC and RPC materials are optimized with various experimental parameters, including pH and response time, to attain optimal sensitivity. As seen in Figs. 6(c) and 7(c), the pH is optimized by varying it from 3 to 9 (pH 3, pH 5, pH 7, and pH 9) in both OPC and RPC materials with known concentrations of copper and bismuth metal ions, respectively. According to this pH study, metal ions precipitate the materials in the basic zone following the creation of metal hydroxides, while hydrogen ions are adsorbed in the low acidic region. Thus, the ideal pH value is thought to be 7. Furthermore, the fluorescence response obtained at different time intervals (Figs. 6(d) and 7(d)) is analysed to assess its stability. For roughly 25–30 min, fluorescence was recorded at 5-min intervals. Its photostability is demonstrated by the response time analysis of OPC and RPC, which reveals no discernible change in its fluorescence intensity. Because of their greater stability, OPC and RPC are suitable for conducting additional experiments or setting up experiments during this time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of OPC (Cu ions) and RPC (Bi ions)
ce:section.ce:para:
Various concentrations of copper ions (2 μM–9 μM) were added to the OPC solution, and different concentrations of bismuth ions (1 μM–9 μM) were added to the RPC solution in order to study the sensitivity of OPC and RPC towards copper and bismuth ions. Thereby, the fluorescence intensity of OPC and RPC steadily declines as the concentration of metal ions increases (Figs. 6(e) and 7(e)). Using the collected data, the relationship between the concentration of metal ions (Cu and Bi) and the fluorescence quenching (I
0
/I) was examined. In this case, I
0 denotes the fluorescence intensity prior to the addition of metal ions, while I denotes the fluorescence intensity after the addition of metal ions. Fig. 6(f) shows a linear calibration curve for copper ion, ranging from 2 μM to 9 μM. The linear regression coefficient, R2 is equivalent to 0.984 in terms of correlation, and the lower detection limit was found to be 0.17 ppm using LOD = 3σ/s, where s is the calibration curve's slope and σ is the blank signal's standard deviation (Liu et al., 2018). With a linear regression coefficient of 0.990, the same is used to determine the limit of detection of bismuth ions, which is 0.08 ppm for a range of 1 μM–9 μM (Fig. 7(f)). As a result, OPC and RPC are highly sensitive to bismuth and copper ions, respectively. Table 1 shows a comparison of detection limits between the reported materials and the OPC and RPC materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorptive removal of copper and bismuth ions
ce:section.ce:para:
To eliminate copper and bismuth ions from the tainted water, the adsorption technique is used. The target analytes in this case are bismuth and copper, which are the adsorbates that need to be adsorbed on OPC and RPC, adsorbents, respectively. Here, the adsorbent's adsorption capacity and adsorption efficiency are determined using the following relations:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isotherm and kinetics study of adsorption
ce:section.ce:para:
The present investigation employed adsorption isotherm models, namely the Freundlich and Langmuir models, to ascertain the adsorbent's adsorption capacity (SenthilKumar et al., 2011). Then, the adsorption behaviour over time was ascertained and adsorption rates of Cu and Bi on OPC and RPC, respectively, were evaluated using the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics models. The linear forms of the Langmuir model (A), the Freundlich model (B), pseudo-first-order kinetics (C), and pseudo-second-order kinetics (D) are represented by the following equations (Kim et al., 2023; Yusuff et al., 2019).

Where K
L stands for Langmuir constant, which is the enthalpy of the adsorption, K
F for Freundlich constant, 1/n for the intensity of adsorption, Q
e and Q
t for the concentrations of adsorbed metal ions (mg/g) on the adsorbents' surface at equilibrium and time t, respectively, and C
e for the equilibrium concentration of adsorbate (metal ions) in the solution (mg/L). Whereas K
2 represents the second-order rate constant per minute, K
1 represents the first-order rate constant per minute.

Table 3 presents the adsorption isotherm parameters derived from experimental data obtained from batch experiments. In this case, OPC and RPC materials are suitable for the Langmuir isotherm and the Freundlich isotherm models, respectively (Figs. 8(c) and 9(c)). As a result, multilayer adsorption occurs when Bi ions are adsorbed on RPC, while monolayer adsorption occurs when Cu ions are adsorbed on OPC (Saadi et al., 2015). Therefore, one of the methods used to remove both metal ions from the contaminated water is physical adsorption (Saadi et al., 2015; Jodeh et al., 2015).

As shown in Figs. 8(d) and 9(d), OPC and RPC fit pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics better than pseudo-first-order kinetics. Table 4 presents the kinetic parameters. If the adsorption experimental data fits with the Freundlich isotherm, physisorption takes place and chemisorption takes place for the experimental data which fits with the Langmuir isotherm and pseudo-second-order kinetics (Kim et al., 2023). Hence, it is feasible to conclude that adsorption happened by both chemisorption and physisorption based on the isotherm and kinetics study. Since polyethlenimine has a large metal ion chelation capability, both sorptions are possible (Ray et al., 2020).

A regeneration experiment is carried out to determine the regeneration efficiency or reusability of OPC and RPC on adsorbing Cu and Bi ions, respectively. Here, three repetitive cycles are performed by turning pH of the solution into a basic region by using NaOH solution. In the basic region, metal ions are precipitated by forming metal hydroxides, which leads to the desorption of heavy metal ions from the surface of the adsorbent. Then the precipitated metal ions are centrifuged and filtered by a 0.22 μm filter, and the samples are allowed to record the concentration of metal ions. Finally, after performing the three cycles, OPC and RPC have the adsorption efficiency of about 86.2 % and 78.2 %, respectively. Hence, both OPC and RPC have better regeneration efficiency (Sherif and Anitha, 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Environmental sample analysis (copper and bismuth ions)
ce:section.ce:para: Table 5 displays the experimental data that illustrate how effectively the nanomaterial (OPC and RPC) recognises and eliminates metal ions (Cu and Bi) from actual samples. After, the preparation of samples as mentioned in section 4.4, the fluorescence intensity of the samples and the final concentration of metal ions after adsorption are determined. Then the fluorescence quenching percentage and adsorption effectiveness of metal-spiked real ambient water samples are examined. Accordingly, the fluorescence quenching percentage rises and the adsorption efficiency falls as the concentration of copper and bismuth ions increases. The real environmental sample analysis also reveals a better recovery percentage of OPC and RPC, when comparing the spiked and determined actual concentration of metal ions. In some cases, the higher recovery percentage occurs due to the presence of some amount of analyte in the real environmental water samples before spiking of the analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism behind sensing and removal of Cu and Bi ions
ce:section.ce:para:
To find and eliminate copper and bismuth ions from the aqueous medium, respectively, fluorescent detection and adsorptive removal techniques are used. When it comes to fluorescence sensing, OPC and RPC encourage the behaviour of turning off fluorescence when exposed to metal ions like bismuth and copper. In this case, material reacting with metal ions causes a quench in fluorescence, which is known as turn-off sensing. Fluorescence can be quenched using either static or dynamic processes (Fraiji et al., 1992). Therefore, a number of investigations, including the measurement of quenching constants, lifetime decay, HRTEM, and zeta potential analysis, are performed in order to determine the appropriate sensing mechanism. The Stern-Volmer (S-V) relation is used to calculate the static quenching constant (K
S ) and dynamic quenching constant (K
D ) as follows. (Choudhury and Mohanty, 2021),
I0/I = 1+(KD + KS)[Q] + (KDKS) [Q]2
τ/τ0 = 1 + KD[Q]
In which I
0 and I are the fluorescence intensities without and with the quencher, respectively, Q is the concentration of the quencher (metal ions), K
S is the static quenching constant, and τ stands for the fluorescence lifetime when the ions are present and τ
0 for the fluorescence lifetime when the ions are absent. On substituting the values, static and dynamic quenching constants are determined for OPC as 4.2 × 104and 24 × 103 respectively. RPC has the static and dynamic quenching constant values as 1.6 × 105 and 37 × 103, respectively. These values indicate that the interaction between the material and metal ion occurs in the ground state rather than the excited state because both OPC and RPC have high static quenching constants (Tanwar et al., 2021). According to lifetime analysis, the electron transfer from the excited carbon dots to the unfilled orbitals of Cu2+ and Bi3+ is indicated by a decrease in the lifetime decay after the incorporation of metal ions. This leads to an effective non-radiative transition and the aggregation-induced emission quenching mechanism (Arumugham et al., 2020). The high static quenching constant value and the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy images of OPC with copper ions (Fig. 10 (a)) and RPC with bismuth ions (Fig. 10(b)) support the aggregation quenching mechanism. Through the chelation effect, the metal ions also attach to hydroxyl groups, such as OH groups, on the surfaces of RPC and OPC, to form complexes (Khan et al., 2019). On the other hand, the sensitivity of RPC's fluorescence spectrum with Bi ions shows the influence of complex formation, indicating both the red shift in their peaks and the quenching of fluorescence (Pandey et al., 2019).

The electrostatic interaction between cationic metal ions and anionic carbon dots is responsible for the adsorptive removal of metal ions. Zeta potential measurements are used to analyse the surface charges of OPC, RPC, Cu, and Bi ions. The results confirmed the anionic nature of OPC (−15.3 mV) and RPC (−3.56 mV), and the cationic nature of Cu (6.5 mV) and Bi (9.1 mV) ions. Therefore, electrostatic contact is the effective mechanism for the adsorption of metal ions (Laptinskiy et al., 2022). As a result, this interaction further validates the isotherm studies, which show that the physisorption process causes the material-metal contact. Moreover, Fig. 4(a) represents the FTIR spectra of the material OPC and RPC, before and after the adsorption process, and it clearly shows there is no significant change in the vibrational spectra, which clearly signifies the stability and reusability of OPC and RPC materials. After examining the mechanism, it is determined that electrostatic contact aids in the adsorptive removal process and that static, aggregation, and chelation mechanisms are mostly responsible for fluorescence sensing.

Successful synthesis of polyethylenimine-assimilated carbon dots (PC) is achieved in the present work. In order to make OPC and RPC with various emission characteristics, the synthesized PC is additionally dispersed in methanol and water. The shape of OPC and RPC carbon dots is spherical, and their respective particle sizes are 7.2 and 8.5 nm. With the use of FTIR, XPS, and EDX analyses, its functional groups and elemental composition are investigated. Additionally, a thorough investigation of PC's solvatochromic effect is carried out. Subsequently, the band gap energy, size, emission colour, and functional groups on the surface of OPC and RPC are studied in relation to the quantum confinement effect and surface state effect on the fluorophore's emission.

OPC and RPC therefore have the ability to detect and eliminate metal ions from the water that has been contaminated. Based on the fluorescence quenching response of the copper and bismuth ions, respectively, OPC and RPC exhibit effective selectivity. Its sensitivity to copper and bismuth ions is also determined, and pH and time are further tuned to improve its efficiency. Thus, the detection limit for bismuth is around 0.08 ppm and the detection limit for copper is 0.17 ppm, both fall below the World Health Organization's (WHO) allowable level (Malik et al., 2019; Fernandez-Luqueno et al., 2013). Executing batch experiments, the adsorption efficiency is increased, and approximately 90 % removal efficiency for both metal ions is attained. Furthermore, adsorption kinetics and isotherm models reveals the nature of adsorption.

A comprehensive discussion of the mechanisms underlying the sensing and removal process indicates that the static, aggregation, and chelation mechanisms serve as the fundamentals of the sensing approach. Materials adsorb metal ions as a result of their electrostatic interactions with one another. In order to improve understanding, these mechanisms are also successfully demonstrated with pertinent outcomes. Finally, prepared OPC and RPC effectively detect and eliminate metal ions from actual ambient water samples.
Nawas Sherif Z: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Anitha K: Writing – review & editing, Validation.
